questions.question: In Unity, what component must every GameObject have?
questions.explanation: Every GameObject has a Transform component (position, rotation, scale). It cannot be removed. Rigidbody, Collider, and Renderer are optional components added as needed.

questions.question: Update() and FixedUpdate() are called at the same rate in Unity.
questions.explanation: Update() is called every frame (variable rate, depends on FPS). FixedUpdate() is called at a fixed interval (default 0.02s = 50Hz) regardless of frame rate. Physics code should go in FixedUpdate() for deterministic behavior.

questions.question: What does this Unity C# code do?
questions.explanation: transform.Translate moves the object. Vector3.forward = (0,0,1). Multiplying by Time.deltaTime makes it frame-rate independent: 5 units per second regardless of FPS. Without deltaTime, speed would vary with frame rate.

questions.question: In Unity, ___ are reusable templates for creating GameObjects at runtime.
questions.explanation: Prefabs are pre-configured GameObject templates stored as assets. Instantiate() creates copies at runtime. Changes to the prefab asset propagate to all instances. Essential for bullets, enemies, pickups — anything spawned dynamically.

questions.question: A Unity character passes through walls despite having a Rigidbody and BoxCollider. What's the bug?
options: Moving via transform.position bypasses physics — collisions are ignored
BoxCollider needs isTrigger = false (might be checked)
speed is too high — object tunnels through thin walls
questions.explanation: Directly setting transform.position teleports the object, bypassing the physics engine. Colliders only respond to physics-driven movement. Fix: use Rigidbody.MovePosition() or rb.velocity for physics-aware movement. This is the #1 Unity physics bug.

questions.question: Your Unity mobile game targets 60fps but drops to 30fps during combat (20 enemies on screen). Profiler shows GC spikes. What's the fix?

scenario.context: Unity 2022, IL2CPP build. Combat spawns enemies, each firing projectiles every 0.5s.

metrics.Main culprits: Instantiate() and string concatenation in UI

options: Object pooling — pre-instantiate projectiles and enemies, recycle instead of Instantiate/Destroy
Use Burst compiler for all scripts
Increase quality settings target frame rate
questions.explanation: Object pooling eliminates GC by recycling objects instead of creating/destroying them. Pre-allocate pools at level start. On 'spawn': enable a pooled object. On 'destroy': disable and return to pool. Eliminates the 2.5MB/frame allocation.

questions.question: Arrange Unity's script lifecycle methods in execution order:
questions.explanation: Unity lifecycle: Awake (first, even if disabled) → OnEnable (when activated) → Start (before first Update, once) → Update (every frame) → LateUpdate (after all Updates, good for camera follow).

questions.question: Match each Unity system to its purpose:

right: Async asset loading with memory management

questions.explanation: ScriptableObjects store data (items, stats) without scene dependency. Addressables manage asset bundles and loading. DOTS/ECS enables massive entity counts with cache-friendly memory. Shader Graph creates shaders visually.

questions.question: You're building a multiplayer Unity game with 50 players. Which networking architecture?
questions.scenario: Action RPG with real-time combat. Need: responsive movement, authoritative hit detection, 50 concurrent players per server.

options.label: Unity Netcode for GameObjects (NGO) with client-authoritative movement
Dedicated server (headless Unity) with server-authoritative physics
Photon PUN relay with room-based architecture
Custom ECS server (non-Unity) with thin Unity client

questions.explanation: For competitive multiplayer with anti-cheat requirements, server-authoritative is necessary. Headless Unity server runs the same game logic, validates all actions, and rejects cheating. Client-side prediction masks latency. NGO or Fish-Net for transport.

questions.question: Unity changed its pricing model to include a per-install fee (Runtime Fee). Is this ethical for the game development ecosystem?
questions.scenario: In 2023, Unity announced a per-install fee for games exceeding revenue/install thresholds. Developers who built studios around Unity's original terms faced unexpected costs. After backlash, terms were modified.

options.label: Unity has the right to change pricing — it's their platform
options.framework: Business prerogative — companies set their own terms

options.label: Retroactive pricing changes violate implicit contracts with existing developers
options.framework: Contract ethics — terms at time of adoption should be honored

options.label: Per-install fees align platform incentives with developer success
options.framework: Alignment — Unity earns when developers earn

options.label: Engine pricing should be open-source or perpetual-license to prevent platform risk

questions.explanation: The backlash centered on retroactive application: developers made business decisions (chose Unity) based on existing terms. Changing terms for already-shipped games violated trust. Unity partially reversed, but the incident accelerated Godot/Unreal adoption.
